pattern: Index every word by all of its (suffix, prefix) joined keys at construction time so a prefix-plus-suffix query becomes one dictionary lookup that returns the largest matching index.
hints: A query pairs a prefix with a suffix; precomputing answers at build time turns each query into a fast lookup.
For each word, enumerate every suffix and every prefix and store a combined key (suffix marker prefix) mapped to that word's index.
Because later words should win ties, overwrite the stored index as you process words in order; a query then builds the same combined key and reads the stored index, or -1 if absent.

edge_inputs.description: single word matched by its prefix and suffix
later duplicate word wins the larger index
no word has both the prefix and suffix
prefix matches but suffix does not
multiple queries against the same dictionary

stress.description: many short words then random prefix/suffix queries
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    letters = 'abc'
    nwords = rng.randint(1, min(size, 60))
    words = [''.join(rng.choice(letters) for _ in range(rng.randint(1, 7))) for _ in range(nwords)]
    ops = ['WordFilter']
    args = [[words]]
    nq = rng.randint(1, min(size, 60))
    for _ in range(nq):
        w = rng.choice(words)
        pref = w[:rng.randint(1, len(w))]
        suff = w[len(w) - rng.randint(1, len(w)):]
        ops.append('f')
        args.append([pref, suff])
    return [ops, args]
stress.note: queries drawn from real words so matches are common; later-index tie-break exercised
